C001.result_snippet: Noether's theorem states that every continuous symmetry of the action of a physical system with conservative forces has a corresponding conservation law. This is the first of two theorems (see Noether's second theorem) published by the mathematician Emmy Noether in 1918. More than that, Noether's theorem provides a systematic way to derive these conserved quantities directly from the symmetries. Many familiar conservation laws in physics correspond precisely to symmetries of the underlying dynamics. Through symmetry of the action under global spacetime translations, Noether's first theorem infamously entails an energy-momentum tensor (EMT) that is neither symmetric nor gauge-invariant. Emmy Noether showed that fundamental physical laws are just a consequence of simple symmetries.Noether discovered that conservation laws aren’t fundamental axioms of the universe. Instead, they emerge from deeper symmetries. Physical laws exhibit symmetry when they remain unchanged under certain transformations. Here are key examples: Time translation symmetry: Physical laws don’t change with time. An experiment performed today gives the same results as the same experiment tomorrow. Noether's second theorem generalizes point 3: gauge symmetries of the action correspond to redundant Euler-Lagrange equations, which lead to undetermined time evolution. The simplest example of Noether's second theorem in field theory is electromagnetism.
